label: Mises's regression theorem and Bitcoin
brief:
Mises proposed the regression theorem to solve a chicken-and-egg problem: why are people willing to accept money today? Because they expect it to have purchasing power tomorrow. Why do they expect that? Because it had purchasing power yesterday. Trace this regress backward and you must eventually reach a moment when the good was demanded for some non-monetary reason — gold's industrial and ornamental uses, for instance — and only then bootstrapped into monetary use [1]. Mises's claim is that this is logically necessary: a thing cannot acquire monetary value out of thin air.

For most of Bitcoin's first decade, this was the strongest objection from Austrian economists. Bitcoin had no prior non-monetary use. It seemed to bootstrap into purchasing power from nothing. Some Austrians concluded that Bitcoin therefore could not be money in Mises's sense and would eventually collapse [2].

The Konrad Graf reading, which has become the dominant Austrian response to the objection, is more careful. The regression theorem requires that there be some prior demand grounded in something other than a 'previously established purchasing power' — but it does not require that demand to be physical or industrial [3]. Bitcoin's earliest demand came from cypherpunks who valued it for its censorship-resistance, its programmability, and as a curiosity to be hashed and traded for fun. That non-monetary demand was real, even if it was abstract. The first Bitcoin transactions — Hal Finney receiving 10 BTC from Satoshi as a test, the famous pizza purchase — were not yet monetary in the strict sense; they were technological experiments that grounded a later monetary use [3].

Once that bootstrap was complete, the regression terminates correctly: Bitcoin had subjective value before it had monetary value, and so satisfies the theorem on a careful reading. The theorem is preserved; the objection dissolves. Whether you find this persuasive depends on how strictly you read 'non-monetary demand' — but the engineering itself does not violate the underlying logic.

sources.label: Ludwig von Mises — The Theory of Money and Credit
sources.excerpt: The objective exchange-value of money which rules in the market today is derived from yesterday's under the influence of the subjective valuations of the persons taking part in transactions on the market.

sources.label: Mises Institute Wire — Bitcoin and Austrian Economics
sources.excerpt: Some Austrians argue that Bitcoin cannot satisfy the regression theorem and therefore cannot become money in the technical sense; others have shown that the theorem applies more loosely than the strict reading suggests.

sources.label: Konrad S. Graf — The Mises Regression Theorem and Bitcoin
sources.excerpt: Bitcoin's earliest direct demand came from those who valued the system itself — its open-source code, its censorship resistance, the elegance of the consensus mechanism. This non-monetary appraisal grounded the bootstrap into monetary use, exactly as the theorem requires.
